title: Cock-a-leekie
subtitle: Chicken and leek broth with prunes
excerpt: A clear Scottish broth of chicken simmered with leeks until tender, finished with a few prunes in the traditional manner. Simple and warming.
sourceNotes: Cock-a-leekie is one of the oldest documented Scottish soups. F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), traces its recorded history to the sixteenth century and includes the prune version. Theodora FitzGibbon, A Taste of Scotland (1970), also documents the dish. The combination of leeks and prunes is the historically traditional version, though many modern versions omit the prunes.

content.text: Cock-a-leekie is one of the oldest Scottish soups: chicken, leeks, and water, lightly seasoned. The prunes are traditional. They add a quiet sweetness that rounds the broth without tasting like fruit.

The original version was made with a whole old boiling fowl, which produced a richer broth. Chicken thighs are the practical modern substitute. They must go in raw and whole so the bone contributes to the stock. Roast chicken leftovers can be used, but the broth will be noticeably lighter.

items.name: Leek
items.prepNote: trimmed, washed; 2 roughly chopped for the stock, 2 sliced into rounds for the soup

items.name: Flat-leaf parsley
items.prepNote: stalks tied for the stock, leaves chopped to finish

content.text: Place the chicken thighs in a large stockpot and cover with {{water}} of cold water. Bring to the boil, then skim thoroughly for 5 to 10 minutes. Add the roughly chopped leeks, the halved onion, parsley stalks, thyme, and bay leaves. If using pearl barley, add it now. Season with salt. Partially cover and simmer gently over low heat for 1.5 hours until the chicken is very tender.

Lift out the chicken thighs and set aside to cool slightly. Strain the broth through a sieve, discarding the aromatics. Return the clear broth to the pot. Remove the chicken meat from the bones in large pieces, discarding the skin and bones. Return the chicken to the broth.
Add the sliced leeks and {{prunes}} stoned prunes if using. Simmer for 15 minutes until the leeks are tender. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Scatter with chopped parsley and serve in deep bowls.

items.fix: Keep the heat at the gentlest simmer throughout. Cloudy broth tastes the same but loses the characteristic clarity of a well-made cock-a-leekie.
items.cause: Insufficient skimming, or the stock boiled hard

items.fix: Braise at the lowest possible heat. Chicken thighs are forgiving but will dry out if boiled rather than simmered.
items.cause: The thighs were cooked at too high a temperature
items.symptom: Chicken meat is dry and stringy

items.fix: Use fewer prunes next time, 8 is enough for a subtle background note. They should not be identifiable as fruit in the finished broth.
items.cause: Too many prunes, or very sweet prunes
items.symptom: Prune flavour is too sweet or dominant

items.fix: Bone-in, skin-on thighs are essential. Season the water generously with salt before simmering.
items.cause: Chicken thighs were skinless and boneless, or water was not adequately seasoned

content.text: Cock-a-leekie has been made in Scotland since at least the sixteenth century, making it one of the oldest named soups in the British Isles. Food writers note it was served at formal Scottish banquets and on everyday tables alike. The prune version reflects a medieval sweet-savoury style common in northern European cooking; it survived in Scotland long after it fell out of fashion elsewhere. The name originally referred to a cock, an old male bird, which gave richer broth but needed far longer cooking.
